serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve carton of packaged soy milk in Singapore is approximately 250 ml (250 g), commonly consumed as a breakfast drink or snack beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Soy Milk (Packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged soy milk (D02030009). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this canonical Singapore source.

gi_source.title: Soy milk, full-fat — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 34 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for full-fat soy milk, which is the closest approved analogous entry to packaged sweetened soy milk. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (34) × carbohydrate per serving (16.5 g from 250 g serving × 6.59 g/100 g) / 100 = 5.6. The sweetened variant may have a marginally higher GI than unsweetened; this estimate is conservative and consistent with published soy milk GI ranges (30–44).

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.15 g/100 g) in a liquid beverage form independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite very low sodium (35 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should choose unsweetened soy milk (sugar ~0 g/100 g) to avoid the sugar-driven blood pressure risk associated with sugar-sweetened beverages, as per AHA 2018 guidance on dietary sugar and cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar content of 5.15 g/100 g in liquid form with minimal dietary fibre (0.37 g/100 g) results in rapid postprandial glucose absorption, making this sweetened packaged variant unsuitable for blood glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid sweetened packaged soy milk and instead select unsweetened variants (no added sugar label) to benefit from soy protein without the glycaemic burden of added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Soy protein (2.57 g/100 g) and isoflavones in soy milk have demonstrated modest LDL-cholesterol-lowering effects; saturated fat is low (estimated 0.26 g/100 g) and cholesterol is absent, but added sugar (5.15 g/100 g) partially offsets cardiovascular benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Packaged sweetened soy milk is acceptable in moderation for cardiovascular health given its low saturated fat and soy protein content, but unsweetened variants are preferred to maximise the cardioprotective benefit of soy isoflavones without the added sugar load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (5.15 g/100 g) without meaningful fibre offset (0.37 g/100 g) does not meet the anti-inflammatory dietary criterion; unsweetened soy milk with isoflavones would be preferable.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose unsweetened soy milk to obtain the anti-inflammatory benefits of soy isoflavones without the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugars.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged sweetened soy milk (豆浆, Susu Soya) is a ready-to-drink plant-based beverage made from soybeans, water, and added sugar, widely consumed across Singapore and Southeast Asia as a breakfast drink, snack, or dairy milk alternative. It is produced by soaking, grinding, and boiling soybeans, then filtering the liquid and adding sugar before packaging. The beverage has a mild, slightly beany flavour with a smooth, thin consistency and a light sweetness. Per 100 g, it provides 52 kcal, 2.57 g protein, 1.77 g fat, 6.59 g carbohydrate (of which 5.15 g is sugar), 0.37 g dietary fibre, 35 mg sodium, 82 mg calcium, and 0.41 mg iron. The GI is approximately 34 (low), but the added sugar content (5.15 g/100 g) in liquid form raises postprandial glucose risk for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and blood pressure risk for those with hypertension, as liquid sugars are absorbed more rapidly than solid food sugars. Unsweetened soy milk is clinically preferred for diabetes and hypertension management. Soy protein and isoflavones in this beverage offer modest cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. It is vegan, dairy-free, gluten-free, and halal-suitable. Common in Singapore hawker centres, supermarkets, and convenience stores, often sold in 250 ml cartons or bottles.
common_user_queries: Is packaged soy milk good for diabetes?
Is soy milk suitable for hypertension?
How many calories in packaged soy milk?
What is the GI of soy milk?
Soy milk vs dairy milk nutrition

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (D02030009), which is the primary canonical source for Singapore food records. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database as an analogous estimate for full-fat soy milk (GI 34), consistent with published literature range of 30–44 for soy milk. Confidence is high for macronutrients and secondary nutrients; GI is analogous estimate with documented basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific packaged soy milk entry are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (D02030009) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (0.26 g/100 g) and potassium (118 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for soy milk (NDB 16222) as the closest approved secondary source, and are documented as adapted values. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg/100 g) as soy milk is a plant-based beverage containing no animal-derived cholesterol. GI value of 34 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for full-fat soy milk; the sweetened packaged variant may have a marginally higher GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 250 g serving size: GL = 34 × (6.59 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.6. Input record health_suitability conditions used 'diabetes' and 'hypertension' keys; these have been mapped to 'type2_diabetes' and 'hypertension' per Veritas v2 naming rules. The condition name 'Type 2 Diabetes' is used in human_condition_name per project requirements.
